content.text: When an enterprise routes proprietary data — customer records, financial projections, legal drafts, or clinical notes — through a third-party LLM API, that vendor becomes a data processor with direct access to some of the most sensitive assets in the organization. Treating the selection of an LLM vendor as a commodity purchase is a governance failure waiting to happen. LLM vendor security due diligence is the structured process of verifying, before contract signature, that a vendor's security posture, compliance certifications, data-handling practices, and API controls meet the risk tolerance of the consuming organization.
What It Is

LLM vendor security due diligence is a systematic assessment framework applied to any AI inference provider, model-as-a-service platform, or LLM fine-tuning service before the organization begins routing production data through that vendor's infrastructure. The process has six principal workstreams:
Security questionnaire completion
— the vendor completes a standardized control inventory (SIG Core, SIG Lite, or CAIQ) covering access management, cryptography, incident response, and supply chain risk.
Certification and audit review

— independent confirmation that the vendor holds current SOC 2 Type II and ISO/IEC 27001 certifications from accredited auditors, with review of the bridging period and any exceptions noted in the audit opinion.
Data residency and jurisdictional analysis

— confirming where inference, fine-tuning, and logging infrastructure is physically hosted and whether cross-border data transfers are adequately governed under GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent mechanisms.
Penetration test and vulnerability disclosure review
— evaluating the recency and scope of third-party penetration tests and assessing whether the vendor operates a credible Vulnerability Disclosure Program (VDP) with documented response timelines.
Model training data provenance

— establishing that the vendor has not used customer prompt or completion data for model training without explicit consent, and that the vendor can provide an auditable lineage for the base model's training corpus.
API security controls inventory
— verifying transport security (TLS 1.2+ minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred), API key rotation policies, rate limiting, secrets management guidance, and availability of structured audit log export to a SIEM.

Unlike traditional SaaS vendor assessments, LLM due diligence must additionally address model-specific risks: prompt injection via the vendor's own safety layers, undisclosed model versioning that silently changes output behavior, and the possibility that fine-tuning jobs cause training data from one customer to leak into another customer's model weights.
Why It Matters

Three converging pressures make thorough LLM vendor due diligence non-negotiable in enterprise deployments. First, the data surface area is large: a single LLM API call can contain an entire customer support ticket, a legal contract clause, or a patient's medication history. Second, LLM infrastructure is novel enough that many providers lack the operational security maturity of established cloud hyperscalers — trust portal documentation may be recent, SOC 2 reports may cover a narrow scope, and incident response processes may be underdeveloped. Third, regulatory risk is increasing: GDPR enforcement actions for AI processors are active, the EU AI Act creates tiered obligations for high-risk AI systems, and US state privacy laws are multiplying. Organizations that skip due diligence face contract liability, regulatory fines, and reputational damage when vendor incidents expose customer data.

A practical example: if a vendor's infrastructure is breached and your organization's prompt data — which contained PII — was retained in inference logs beyond the contractually agreed period, your organization is jointly accountable under GDPR Article 82 (liability of the controller) even though the breach occurred on the vendor's systems. Proper due diligence documents the vendor's commitments, giving you the contractual basis to enforce them and potentially limit your regulatory exposure.
Benefits
Risk quantification before commitment
— scoring frameworks produce a numeric risk signal that procurement, legal, and CISO teams can compare against organizational thresholds, making go/no-go decisions defensible.
Contractual leverage

— documented gaps discovered during diligence become negotiation points: you can require a GDPR Data Processing Agreement (DPA), specific data deletion timelines, or a right-to-audit clause before signing.
Reduced incident blast radius
— knowing a vendor's incident response SLA and notification procedures allows your team to prepare playbooks, reducing response time when (not if) a vendor security event occurs.
Regulatory audit trail

— a timestamped evidence dossier (certifications, questionnaire responses, pen test summaries) satisfies the Article 28 requirement to maintain records of processing activities and demonstrates due diligence to regulators.
Vendor accountability baseline
— the same evidence collection process used during initial diligence becomes the template for annual re-assessments, creating a continuous monitoring loop rather than a one-time checkbox exercise.
Competitive intelligence
— comparing security postures across shortlisted vendors often reveals meaningful differences in maturity that are not visible from marketing materials alone.
Trade-offs
LLM vendor due diligence is a meaningful investment that must be sized proportionally to the data sensitivity and contract value involved.
Trade-off
Detail
Mitigation
Time to onboarding
Full SIG assessments can take 4–8 weeks for both sides; slow-moving vendor trust portals compound the delay
Use tiered assessment: SIG Lite for low-risk vendors, full SIG Core + on-site for high-sensitivity use cases; automate evidence collection via trust portal APIs
LLM-specific gaps
Standard SIG/CAIQ questionnaires do not include AI-specific questions about model training data use, inference log retention, or model versioning
Append a custom AI addendum covering training data opt-out, prompt retention policy, silent model update notification, and output filtering auditability
Point-in-time certificates
SOC 2 reports and ISO 27001 certificates reflect a past audit period; vendor posture may have degraded since issuance
Require continuous monitoring: subscribe to vendor security advisories, schedule annual re-assessments, and contractually require 30-day notification of material security changes
Vendor non-disclosure
Hyperscale LLM providers may refuse to share pen test reports, citing NDA or competitive sensitivity
Request executive summaries with remediation evidence; treat full refusal as a yellow flag in scoring; escalate to right-to-audit clause negotiation
How It Works

The due diligence process follows a weighted evidence accumulation model. Each control domain is assigned a maximum point value based on its risk relevance to LLM deployments. Evidence is collected from three sources — vendor-submitted questionnaire responses, independently issued third-party certifications, and automated retrieval from the vendor's trust portal — and then scored against domain-specific rubrics. The aggregate score determines one of three outcomes:
Pass (score ≥ 75)
— vendor meets baseline requirements; proceed to contractual review and DPA negotiation.
Conditional Pass (50 ≤ score < 75)
— vendor has material gaps; proceed only with a time-bound remediation plan, compensating controls, or data minimization constraints (e.g., no PII through this vendor until gaps close).
Fail (score < 50)
— do not proceed with production data. Re-assess after vendor demonstrates remediation or move to an alternative vendor.

The weighting schema reflects the specific threat model for LLM inference services. SOC 2 Type II carries the highest weight (25 points) because it provides independent attestation of the operational security controls most relevant to data handling — logical access, availability, change management, and incident response — over a minimum 12-month audit period. ISO 27001 (20 points) adds a process-management lens: it attests that the vendor has a functioning Information Security Management System (ISMS), not just a point-in-time configuration. Data residency (15 points) and penetration testing (15 points) together address the two most common LLM-specific risk vectors: cross-border data exposure and exploitable vulnerabilities in the inference API surface.
Architecture

attrs.alt: LLM vendor security due diligence pipeline: Vendor Discovery → Security Questionnaire (SIG/CAIQ) → Certification Review (SOC2/ISO27001) → Data Residency Check → Pen Test Review → Risk Scoring → Approval Decision, with supporting detail boxes for evidence collection, compliance controls, API security controls, and risk score weights

content.text: The pipeline is linear but evidence collection for multiple stages happens in parallel. Vendor Discovery produces the initial shortlist and triggers two concurrent workstreams: the vendor-facing questionnaire process and the automated evidence collection from the vendor's public trust portal. Certification Review gates on independently verifiable documents (SOC 2 report, ISO 27001 certificate) that arrive on the vendor's schedule, so automated polling of trust portal APIs reduces manual tracking overhead. All collected evidence feeds the Risk Scoring engine, which produces the final numeric score and the structured scorecard that the Approval Decision relies on. The scorecard also doubles as the compliance artifact stored in your organization's vendor management system.
When to Use It
Apply this framework whenever the following conditions hold:
Production data will flow through the vendor's inference API
— even for anonymous-seeming use cases, metadata leakage (IP address, user agent, request timing) is sufficient for re-identification in some regulatory contexts.
The use case involves regulated data categories
— PII under GDPR/CCPA, PHI under HIPAA, financial data under PCI-DSS, or any data subject to sector-specific regulations mandates a formal assessment.
Fine-tuning or retrieval-augmented generation on proprietary corpora
— when the vendor stores training data or document chunks on their infrastructure, the data residency and training-data-use provisions become critical.
The agentic system takes consequential actions
— if the LLM can write to databases, send emails, or call external APIs on behalf of users, a compromise of the inference layer has elevated blast radius and warrants more rigorous assessment.
Contract value or data volume crosses internal thresholds
— most organizations have risk-tiered vendor management programs; LLM API contracts above a defined spend threshold or data volume automatically trigger the full due diligence workstream.

The assessment is not required — and often counterproductive — for purely internal model deployments (e.g., self-hosted open weights models on private compute) where no data leaves the organization's trust boundary. In those cases, the equivalent security work is an internal deployment review focused on the model's training data provenance and the infrastructure hosting it.
How to Implement

The implementation has two components: an automated evidence collector that retrieves documents from the vendor's trust portal API, and a scoring engine that converts evidence into a risk score. Starting with the evidence collector:

"""Automated LLM vendor evidence collection from trust portal APIs."""
import asyncio
import datetime
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import httpx


@dataclass
class VendorEvidence:
    vendor_id: str
    collected_at: str = field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    )
    soc2_report_date: Optional[str] = None
    soc2_scope: Optional[str] = None
    iso27001_valid_until: Optional[str] = None
    iso27001_accreditation_body: Optional[str] = None
    pen_test_date: Optional[str] = None
    pen_test_scope: Optional[str] = None
    pen_test_provider: Optional[str] = None
    data_residency_regions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    gdpr_dpa_available: bool = False
    ccpa_addendum_available: bool = False
    training_data_opt_out: bool = False
# ... (truncated)

The scoring engine applies the weighting schema and emits a structured result. Critical controls that are absent — a missing SOC 2 report, or evidence that the vendor retains prompts indefinitely — should be flagged as blockers even when the numeric score is above the pass threshold:

"""LLM vendor security due diligence scorer with weighted criteria."""
import datetime
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any


# Domain weights — must sum to 100
CRITERIA_WEIGHTS: dict[str, int] = {
    "soc2_type2": 25,
    "iso27001": 20,
    "data_residency": 15,
    "pen_test": 15,
    "gdpr_ccpa": 10,
    "api_security": 10,
    "vdp": 5,
}

PASS_THRESHOLD = 75
CONDITIONAL_PASS_THRESHOLD = 50


@dataclass
class DiligenceResult:
    vendor_name: str
    score: float
# ... (truncated)
Examples
The following example shows how the scorer is invoked with a realistic evidence dictionary, covering both a passing vendor and a vendor with material gaps:

"""Scoring examples — passing vendor and failing vendor."""
from scorer import LLMVendorDiligenceScorer

scorer = LLMVendorDiligenceScorer(required_data_regions=["EU", "US"])

# --- Passing vendor example ---
acme_evidence = {
    "soc2_report_date": "2024-10-15",         # 9 months ago — current
    "soc2_scope": "API inference infrastructure and key management",
    "iso27001_valid_until": "2026-03-01",      # well within validity window
    "iso27001_accreditation_body": "BSI Group",
    "pen_test_date": "2025-01-20",             # 6 months ago
    "pen_test_scope": "External API and web application",
    "pen_test_provider": "NCC Group",
    "data_residency_regions": ["EU", "US"],
    "gdpr_dpa_available": True,
    "ccpa_addendum_available": True,
    "training_data_opt_out": True,
    "prompt_retention_days": 0,                # zero-retention option
    "vdp_url": "https://vendor.com/security",
    "vdp_response_sla_days": 2,
    "api_tls_minimum": "TLS1.3",
    "api_key_rotation_days": 90,
}

# ... (truncated)
The third example shows the structured vendor scorecard JSON that the scorer should serialize and store as a compliance artifact in your vendor management system:

{
  "vendor_name": "Acme LLM Inc.",
  "assessed_at": "2025-07-12T09:00:00",
  "assessor": "security-team@yourorg.com",
  "score": 100.0,
  "status": "pass",
  "domain_scores": {
    "soc2_type2": 25.0,
    "iso27001": 20.0,
    "data_residency": 15.0,
    "pen_test": 15.0,
    "gdpr_ccpa": 10.0,
    "api_security": 10.0,
    "vdp": 5.0
  },
  "blockers": [],
  "notes": [],
  "evidence_summary": {
    "soc2_report_date": "2024-10-15",
    "soc2_scope": "API inference infrastructure and key management",
    "iso27001_valid_until": "2026-03-01",
    "iso27001_accreditation_body": "BSI Group",
    "pen_test_date": "2025-01-20",
    "pen_test_provider": "NCC Group",
    "data_residency_regions": ["EU", "US"],
# ... (truncated)
Real-World Example

Consider a global financial services firm evaluating an LLM vendor for a document summarization use case where the documents contain non-public information (MNPI) and client PII. The diligence team discovers through the questionnaire process that the vendor's SOC 2 report covers only "corporate IT infrastructure" and explicitly excludes the inference API tier. The certification exists, but its scope is misaligned with the actual processing surface.

The risk scorer would award partial SOC 2 points (reflecting that some operational controls are audited) but flag a note requiring the vendor to extend audit scope to cover the inference API in the next report cycle. More critically, the scorer would lower the data residency score when querying the trust portal reveals that the vendor's API gateway processes requests in a US-West region by default, with no EU-primary option — a problem for EU-resident client data under GDPR Article 44 transfer restrictions.

The resulting conditional pass (score: 61) triggers a remediation plan with three conditions: (1) the vendor must provide a written commitment to extend SOC 2 scope to cover inference infrastructure by the next audit cycle; (2) the organization will use the vendor's EU-region endpoint for all requests containing EU-resident PII; (3) a GDPR DPA with Standard Contractual Clauses covering US data transfers must be executed before go-live. The financial services firm proceeds to production with PII data only after all three conditions are satisfied — documented in the scorecard artifact. The entire decision trail is preserved for the next regulatory audit.
Common Mistakes
Accepting a SOC 2 Type I instead of Type II

— Type I attests that controls are suitably designed at a point in time; Type II attests that controls operated effectively over a period of at least six months. For production systems, only Type II is meaningful.
Ignoring the SOC 2 scope description
— a vendor may hold a valid SOC 2 Type II report that covers only their corporate HR and billing systems, not their inference API. Always read the scope section.
Treating prompt retention as a billing concern rather than a security concern

— many organizations focus on cost optimization in vendor negotiations and overlook prompt retention entirely. A vendor retaining prompts for 12 months creates a one-year window of data exposure in the event of a breach.
Conflating model privacy with infrastructure security
— a vendor may genuinely not train on your prompts (model privacy) while still retaining prompt logs on insecure infrastructure (infrastructure security). Both must be assessed independently.
Skipping annual re-assessment

— initial diligence is not a permanent clearance. LLM vendor security postures change rapidly as companies scale, pivot infrastructure, or are acquired. Build annual re-assessment into vendor management workflows.
Applying the same framework to all use cases

— a vendor used only for internal knowledge-base Q&A over non-sensitive documentation does not require the same rigour as one processing customer PII. Risk-tier your assessments to avoid assessment fatigue.
Not requesting DPA before data transfer

— under GDPR Article 28, a Data Processing Agreement must be in place before a controller routes personal data to a processor. Sending even a single API request containing personal data before the DPA is signed constitutes a compliance violation.
Best Practices
Use SIG Lite as the intake filter
— send the 120-question SIG Lite to all candidate vendors simultaneously. Use the responses to eliminate obviously unsuitable vendors before investing time in full SIG Core or on-site reviews.
Automate trust portal polling

— most leading LLM providers expose machine-readable certification metadata at a trust portal. Integrate polling into your vendor management platform to detect certificate expirations or report staleness automatically, rather than relying on manual calendar reminders.
Negotiate a right-to-audit clause

— even if you never exercise it, the clause changes the vendor's incentives by making it clear that security claims may be independently verified. Include it in MSA negotiations for high-sensitivity use cases.
Separate data minimization from due diligence

— due diligence assesses the vendor; data minimization (PII redaction, tokenization, synthetic data generation before the API call) reduces the data your organization sends to the vendor. Both are necessary and complementary; neither is a substitute for the other.
Maintain a blockers register

— preserve every blocker and its resolution in the vendor record. Blockers that were accepted (with compensating controls) and those that were remediated by the vendor both need to be traceable for auditor inquiries.
Align diligence scope with data classification

— map your organization's data classification tiers (public, internal, confidential, restricted) to minimum vendor score thresholds. A vendor with a score of 65 may be acceptable for internal-classification workloads but not for restricted-classification data.
Track model version change notifications

— contractually require the vendor to notify you at least 14 days before a silent model update to a production endpoint. Unexpected model changes can alter output behavior in ways that invalidate safety validations you performed on the prior model version.
Performance & Scalability

The due diligence process itself has operational performance characteristics that matter in organizations managing large vendor portfolios. A full SIG Core assessment involves 320–700 questions across 18 domains and typically requires 8–20 hours of effort from the vendor's security team and 4–8 hours of review from the buyer. At scale, this creates a backlog problem: if your organization is evaluating five LLM vendors simultaneously for three separate business units, the manual questionnaire process consumes significant security team capacity.
Three techniques reduce this overhead without compromising rigor:
Evidence re-use

— SOC 2 reports, ISO 27001 certificates, and pen test summaries are valid for 12 months. A vendor assessed six months ago for a different business unit does not need a full re-assessment; the stored evidence dossier can be reviewed for staleness and reused.
Tiered depth

— SIG Lite (120 questions) takes approximately one quarter of the time of SIG Core. Use Lite for vendors processing low-sensitivity data and reserve Core for high-sensitivity or high-volume use cases.
Automated trust portal integration

— the async evidence collector shown earlier can run on a nightly schedule against all active vendors, surfacing certificate expirations and stale pen test reports before they become blockers during a re-assessment review.

For organizations with dozens of active LLM API integrations, integrating evidence collection with a GRC platform (OneTrust, ServiceNow GRC, Vanta, Drata) allows continuous control monitoring against the same scoring rubric, replacing annual point-in-time assessments with real-time posture visibility.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Category
Tools / Resources
Notes
Questionnaire frameworks
SIG Core, SIG Lite (Shared Assessments), CAIQ (CSA), VSA (Vendor Security Alliance)
SIG and CAIQ are the dominant standards for enterprise vendor assessments
GRC platforms
OneTrust, ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate
Store evidence dossiers and automate re-assessment scheduling
Continuous compliance
Vanta, Drata, Secureframe
Automate evidence collection from vendor trust portals; integrate with SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits
Vendor trust portals
Anthropic trust.anthropic.com, OpenAI trust.openai.com, Google Cloud compliance.google
Primary source for certifications, pen test summaries, DPA templates, and data residency documentation
API security testing
Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, Postman Security, 42Crunch
Useful for validating TLS enforcement, header security, and rate limiting at integration time
AI-specific frameworks
NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act Annex IV, CSA AI Safety Initiative
Supplement standard cybersecurity questionnaires with AI-specific risk categories not covered by SIG/CAIQ
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Depth
Coverage
Best for
Weighted scoring (this approach)
High
All security domains + AI-specific
Production LLM APIs processing sensitive data; regulatory environments
Standard SaaS security review
Medium
IT security; misses AI-specific risks
Non-AI vendors; procurement teams without AI expertise

Low
Only audited controls; no scope or timeliness check
Initial screening only; insufficient for production decisions

External attack surface; no inside-out visibility

Security & Reliability

Beyond the due diligence of the vendor's own security posture, the integration architecture between your system and the LLM API introduces its own security surface area that must be hardened independently of the vendor's controls.
API key management

— never embed LLM API keys in source code or environment variables in long-running containers. Use a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager) with short-lived dynamic credentials where the vendor supports it. Rotate keys at or below the vendor's recommended rotation interval.
Prompt injection hardening

— treat user-provided content that flows into a prompt as untrusted input. Enforce structural separation between system prompt and user content, and validate that user content cannot escape its intended scope by including instruction-like text. This is an application-layer control that the vendor's security posture cannot substitute for.
Circuit breaker for vendor availability

— model the LLM vendor API as an external dependency that will occasionally be unavailable. Implement timeouts, retries with exponential backoff, and a fallback path (degraded mode, queue for later processing, or secondary vendor) for production agentic workflows where latency or downtime has user-facing impact.
Audit logging of LLM calls

— maintain your own immutable log of every LLM API call: timestamp, model version, user identity (or session token), prompt hash, response hash, and latency. This log is independent of the vendor's retention and is essential for incident investigation and regulatory audit.
Output validation before consequential actions

— in agentic systems that act on LLM output (sending emails, writing to databases, calling downstream APIs), validate structured outputs against a schema before execution. A schema parse failure should halt the action and route to a human review queue.
Mental Model

Think of selecting an LLM vendor the way a hospital selects a medical laboratory outsourcing partner. The laboratory never sees the patient — only the biological sample — but the sample contains highly sensitive personal health information that, if mishandled, causes direct patient harm and regulatory liability. The hospital does not simply trust that the laboratory has good practices; it requires Joint Commission accreditation (the equivalent of SOC 2 Type II), verifies that samples are processed only in approved facilities (data residency), confirms that results are returned and destroyed according to a documented retention policy (prompt retention), and validates that the laboratory has professional liability insurance and an incident response protocol (VDP and IR SLA).

The LLM vendor relationship is structurally identical. Your data — the "sample" — is sent to the vendor's infrastructure for processing and returned as model output. The vendor's security posture determines how well that sample is protected in transit and at rest. Due diligence is your accreditation check. Unlike a laboratory test, however, the LLM vendor's "processing" can leak information across customers through shared infrastructure (cross-tenant isolation failures), and the model itself can be a channel of data leakage if the vendor uses inference data for training. These AI-specific risks explain why LLM vendor due diligence extends beyond the standard IT vendor security checklist.
Key Takeaways

LLM vendor due diligence combines standard IT vendor assessment (SIG/CAIQ, SOC 2, ISO 27001, pen testing, VDP) with AI-specific controls covering model training data use, prompt retention, and model versioning change management.

SOC 2 Type II is the single highest-weighted criterion because it provides independent attestation of operational security controls over time, not just at a point in time — always verify the report covers the inference API scope.

A GDPR Article 28 Data Processing Agreement must be executed before the first API call containing personal data — this is a legal requirement, not a best practice, and is a blocker in the scoring framework.
The three-tier outcome (pass / conditional pass / fail) and the blockers mechanism ensure that critical gaps cause an absolute stop even when the overall numeric score is above threshold.

Automating evidence collection from trust portal APIs converts annual manual reviews into continuous posture monitoring, dramatically reducing the operational cost of managing a multi-vendor LLM portfolio.

Data minimization — PII redaction or tokenization before the API call — and vendor due diligence are complementary, not substitutes: minimize the data you send and verify the security of wherever it goes.

In agentic deployments, the security review must extend beyond the vendor's infrastructure to include your own integration: API key management, prompt injection defenses, circuit breakers, and independent audit logging of every LLM call.
